statement: Anthropic's lead research agent saves its plan to memory because contexts over 200k tokens get truncated.
quote: The LeadResearcher begins by thinking through the approach and saving its plan to Memory to persist the context, since if the context window exceeds 200,000 tokens it will be truncated and it is important to retain the plan.
sourceQuote: The LeadResearcher begins by thinking through the approach and saving its plan to Memory to persist the context, since if the context window exceeds 200,000 tokens it will be truncated and it is important to retain the plan.

statement: Anthropic embeds explicit fan-out sizing rules in prompts: 1 agent for simple lookups, 2-4 for comparisons, 10+ for complex research.
quote: Simple fact-finding requires just 1 agent with 3-10 tool calls, direct comparisons might need 2-4 subagents with 10-15 calls each, and complex research might use more than 10 subagents with clearly divided responsibilities.
sourceQuote: Simple fact-finding requires just 1 agent with 3-10 tool calls, direct comparisons might need 2-4 subagents with 10-15 calls each, and complex research might use more than 10 subagents with clearly divided responsibilities.

statement: Subagent task descriptions require four elements: objective, output format, tool guidance, and boundaries.
quote: Each subagent needs an objective, an output format, guidance on the tools and sources to use, and clear task boundaries.
sourceQuote: Each subagent needs an objective, an output format, guidance on the tools and sources to use, and clear task boundaries.

statement: Two levels of parallelization cut Anthropic's research time by up to 90% on complex queries.
quote: These changes cut research time by up to 90% for complex queries, allowing Research to do more work in minutes instead of hours
sourceQuote: These changes cut research time by up to 90% for complex queries, allowing Research to do more work in minutes instead of hours

statement: Anthropic's LLM judge scores research on five rubric dimensions.
quote: factual accuracy (do claims match sources?), citation accuracy (do the cited sources match the claims?), completeness (are all requested aspects covered?), source quality (did it use primary sources over lower-quality secondary sources?), and tool efficiency (did it use the right tools a reasonable number of times?)
sourceQuote: factual accuracy (do claims match sources?), citation accuracy (do the cited sources match the claims?), completeness (are all requested aspects covered?), source quality (did it use primary sources over lower-quality secondary sources?), and tool efficiency (did it use the right tools a reasonable number of times?)

statement: Citation attribution happens in a dedicated agent pass after the research loop ends.
quote: the system exits the research loop and passes all findings to a CitationAgent, which processes the documents and research report to identify specific locations for citations.
sourceQuote: the system exits the research loop and passes all findings to a CitationAgent, which processes the documents and research report to identify specific locations for citations.

statement: A 302 response makes browsers automatically re-request the resource at the Location header URL.
quote: A browser receiving this status will automatically request the resource at the URL in the `Location` header, redirecting the user to the new page.
sourceQuote: A browser receiving this status will automatically request the resource at the URL in the `Location` header, redirecting the user to the new page.

statement: 302 transfers no SEO value to the new URL.
quote: Search engines receiving this response will not attribute links to the original URL to the new resource, meaning no SEO value is transferred to the new URL.
sourceQuote: Search engines receiving this response will not attribute links to the original URL to the new resource, meaning no [SEO](/en-US/docs/Glossary/SEO) value is transferred to the new URL.

statement: Per the Fetch Standard, a POST that receives 302 is retried as GET.
quote: when a user agent receives a `302` in response to a POST request, it uses the GET method in the subsequent redirection request, as permitted by the HTTP specification.
sourceQuote: when a user agent receives a `302` in response to a [POST](/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Methods/POST) request, it uses the [GET](/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Methods/GET) method in the subsequent redirection request, as permitted by the HTTP specification.

statement: 307 exists to prevent user agents changing the request method.
quote: To avoid user agents modifying the request, use 307 Temporary Redirect instead, as altering the method after a `307` response is prohibited.
sourceQuote: To avoid user agents modifying the request, use [307 Temporary Redirect](/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Status/307) instead, as altering the method after a `307` response is prohibited.

statement: 303 forces any request method to become GET.
quote: In cases where you want any request method to be changed to GET, use 303 See Other.
sourceQuote: In cases where you want any request method to be changed to [GET](/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Methods/GET), use [303 See

statement: 302 indicates a temporary move to the URL in the Location header.
quote: The HTTP 302 Found redirection response status code indicates that the requested resource has been temporarily moved to the URL in the Location header.

statement: ReAct interleaves reasoning traces and actions so each strengthens the other.
quote: reasoning traces help the model induce, track, and update action plans as well as handle exceptions, while actions allow it to interface with external sources, such as knowledge bases or environments, to gather additional information.
sourceQuote: reasoning traces help the model induce, track, and update action plans as well as handle exceptions, while actions allow it to interface with external sources, such as knowledge bases or environments, to gather additional information.

statement: ReAct reduces hallucination in chain-of-thought via a Wikipedia API.
quote: ReAct overcomes issues of hallucination and error propagation prevalent in chain-of-thought reasoning by interacting with a simple Wikipedia API
sourceQuote: ReAct overcomes issues of hallucination and error propagation prevalent in chain-of-thought reasoning by interacting with a simple Wikipedia API

statement: ReAct beats imitation and RL baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop by 34% and 10% absolute.
quote: On two interactive decision making benchmarks (ALFWorld and WebShop), ReAct outperforms imitation and reinforcement learning methods by an absolute success rate of 34% and 10% respectively
sourceQuote: On two interactive decision making benchmarks (ALFWorld and WebShop), ReAct outperforms imitation and reinforcement learning methods by an absolute success rate of 34% and 10% respectively

statement: ReAct needs only one or two in-context examples.
quote: while being prompted with only one or two in-context examples.
sourceQuote: while being prompted with only one or two in-context examples.

statement: ReAct trajectories are more interpretable than no-reasoning baselines.
quote: generates human-like task-solving trajectories that are more interpretable than baselines without reasoning traces
sourceQuote: generates human-like task-solving trajectories that are more interpretable than baselines without reasoning traces

statement: Before ReAct, reasoning and acting in LLMs were studied separately.
quote: their abilities for reasoning (e.g. chain-of-thought prompting) and acting (e.g. action plan generation) have primarily been studied as separate topics.
sourceQuote: their abilities for reasoning (e.g. chain-of-thought prompting) and acting (e.g. action plan generation) have primarily been studied as separate topics.

statement: The article opens by assuming the reader has written components with Hooks.
quote: You wrote a few components with Hooks. Maybe even a small app.
sourceQuote: You wrote a few components with [Hooks](https://reactjs.org/docs/hooks-intro.html). Maybe even a small app.

statement: The author says useEffect sometimes leaves a nagging sense of missing pieces.
quote: But sometimes when you `useEffect`, the pieces don't quite fit together.
sourceQuote: But sometimes when you `useEffect`, the pieces don’t quite fit together.

statement: The article questions the class-lifecycle analogy.
quote: It seems similar to class lifecycles… but is it really?
sourceQuote: It seems similar to class lifecycles… but is it really?

statement: Replicating componentDidMount is one of the listed reader questions.
quote: How do I replicate `componentDidMount` with `useEffect`?
sourceQuote: How do I replicate `componentDidMount` with `useEffect`?

statement: Data fetching and the empty array are among the listed questions.
quote: How do I correctly fetch data inside `useEffect`? What is `[]`?
sourceQuote: How do I correctly fetch data inside `useEffect`? What is `[]`?

statement: The intro mentions extracting repetitive logic into custom Hooks.
quote: You even made some custom Hooks to extract repetitive logic (300 lines gone!) and showed it off to your colleagues.
sourceQuote: and picked up a few tricks along the way. You even made some [custom Hooks](https://reactjs.org/docs/hooks-custom.html) to extract repetitive logic (300 lines gone!) and showed it off to your colleagues.

statement: Graham says manually recruiting users is the most common unscalable founder task.
quote: The most common unscalable thing founders have to do at the start is to recruit users manually. Nearly all startups have to.
sourceQuote:
The most common unscalable thing founders have to do at the start
is to recruit users manually. Nearly all startups have to.

statement: Founders must go get users rather than wait.
quote: You can't wait for users to come to you. You have to go out and get them.
sourceQuote:
You
can't wait for users to come to you. You have to go out and get
them.

statement: Stripe is cited as famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition.
quote: If anyone could have sat back and waited for users, it was Stripe. But in fact they're famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition.
sourceQuote:
If anyone could have sat
back and waited for users, it was Stripe. But in fact they're
famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition.

statement: YC calls the technique the Collison installation.
quote: At YC we use the term "Collison installation" for the technique they invented.
sourceQuote:
At YC we use the term "Collison
installation" for the technique they invented.

statement: The Collison brothers set users up on the spot.
quote: When anyone agreed to try Stripe they'd say "Right then, give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot.
sourceQuote:
When anyone agreed to try Stripe they'd say
"Right then, give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot.

statement: Graham says at least one founder, usually the CEO, must spend heavily on sales and marketing.
quote: for a startup to succeed, at least one founder (usually the CEO) will have to spend a lot of time on sales and marketing.
sourceQuote:
for a
startup to succeed, at least one founder (usually the CEO) will
have to spend a lot of time on sales and marketing.
